focus_areas: Join ordering strategy: (1) filter customer on c_mktsegment='BUILDING' (20% selectivity, 1.5M→300K), (2) hash-join customer→orders on o_custkey (300K build side, 15M probe side), (3) hash-join result→lineitem on l_orderkey. Avoids large intermediate Cartesian products.
Early filter pushdown: apply c_mktsegment=BUILDING before joining orders to minimize join input from 1.5M to 300K; apply orders.o_orderdate<1995-03-15 (8% selectivity) at scan time

Morsel-driven lineitem scan (60M rows): partition into ~4K morsels for 64 cores; each thread scans lineitem morsels, probes hash table (result of customer⋈orders), outputs matching rows to aggregation stage
Per-morsel partial aggregation for 5M-group output: use compact hash table with robin-hood probing; merge partitions via thread-local buffers to avoid contention
Hash join algorithm: two-phase (build phase on smaller side with synchronization point, probe phase fully parallel)
strategy_notes: Q3 is the hardest query: 3-way join with 5M aggregation groups. Critical insight from analysis: filter customer first (20% selectivity) before joining to orders; this reduces the build-side table from 1.5M to 300K rows. Use hash join (PK-FK relationships indicated in analysis) with small build side (300K customer rows after filter). Morsel-parallelism on lineitem probe (60M rows across 64 cores = ~1M rows per morsel). Two aggregation levels: (1) local hash tables per morsel thread, (2) global merge across thread-local partial results. Avoid full synchronization; use atomic CAS or thread-local buffers. Compile the three-way join into single query-specific code path.
reasoning: TPC-H SF10 is a parallel analytical workload dominated by large table scans (59M-60M rows) with low-cardinality aggregations. GenDB has 64 cores available but current performance is competitive only on Q1 (48ms vs 86ms DuckDB). Q3 (386ms) and Q6 (29.3ms) indicate suboptimal parallelism, join strategy, and filter vectorization. Primary optimization levers: (1) morsel-driven thread parallelism across all three queries, (2) SIMD vectorization for complex predicates (Q6 especially), (3) early filter pushdown in Q3 three-way join, (4) per-morsel partial aggregation to minimize synchronization overhead.
